EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly developing very hot and humid weather, with daytime temperatures reaching the 95 to 100 degree range, brought the heat index to above 100 during the afternoon hours of June 10th through June 12th. The heat came less than two days after a period of well below normal temperatures. Several roads buckled in the Sioux Falls area, and emergency rooms at hospitals reported that an unknown number of people suffered heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching daytime highs of 95 to 100 after a period of cool weather were accompanied by humid conditions, with the heat index rising to a little above 100 degrees. An unknown number of people suffered from heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, as reported by hospital emergency rooms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow in southeast south Dakota was accompanied by north to northwest winds reaching sustained speeds near 30 mph with gusts above 40 mph. The combination of snow and wind caused blizzard conditions from February 2nd into the predawn hours of February 3rd, with widespread visibilities near zero, bringing travel to a halt. The worst conditions were over the far southeast corner of the state where over a foot of snow fell, but enough snow fell to result in the blizzard conditions northwest to near a Springfield to Flandreau line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow combined with north to northwest winds 20 to 35 mph, with gusts over 40 mph, to produce near zero visibilities in falling and blowing snow, mainly over the southeast part of the county. Accumulations varied from around an inch near the northwestern corner of the county to 5 inches over the southeast corner of the county southeast of Trent. Travel was brought to a halt and a few vehicles slid off roads due to the combination of snowy roads and low visibility. Schools and several businesses were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air and northwest winds combined to lower wind chill readings to 35 to 45 degrees below zero in southeast South Dakota...mostly near and north of Interstate 90...from the evening of January 16th to the morning of January 17th. Actual temperatures dropped to 10 to 20 degrees below zero, and northwest winds averaged 10 to 15 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped to the 35 to 45 below zero range for much of the night and morning hours on January 16-17. Northwest winds gusted to around 20 mph early in the event, but generally averaged 10 to 15 mph when the coldest wind chills were reached, during which time actual temperatures were 15 to 20 degrees below zero.

Fog, dense in areas, and freezing drizzle caused a combination of icy surfaces with low visibility in part of southeast South Dakota north of Interstate 90 from the evening of January 6th into the morning of January 7th. The area affected was from South Dakota Highway 34 north to the Huron, De Smet, and Brookings areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of both low visibilities from fog and icy surfaces from freezing drizzle combined to make travel hazardous from the night of January 6th to the early daylight hours of January 7th.

Light to moderate snow fell in part of southeast South Dakota from the evening of January 7th to the afternoon of January 8th. Accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were reported in several areas near the eastern border of the state while accumulations further west were less than 2 inches. Winds were light at the start of the snowfall, then increased to 10 to 20 mph on January 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches, including 4.0 inches at Beresford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches over a small area in southeast South Dakota during the morning of January 25th. North to northwest winds gusting to near 30 mph near the end of the snowfall caused minor drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches, including 2.5 inches near Mitchell. North to northwest winds gusting to 30 mph caused minor drifting near the end of the snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the region and brought a variety of weather including lightning and thunder, sleet, freezing rain, and heavy snow. The freezing rain and sleet were concentrated across central South Dakota. The mixed bag of weather caused travel problems across the region. Heavy snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches along with icing amounts from a few hundredths up to three tenths of an inch occurred with this storm.  Some of the snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Aberdeen, Leola, and Doland; 7 inches at Chelsea and Raymond; 8 inches at Butler and east of Lowry; 9 inches at Eagle Butte, Bowdle, and Roscoe; 10 inches east of Hosmer and 12 inches at Gettysburg. Onida received a tenth of an inch of ice with three tenths of an inch of ice at Agar.

Strong wind gusts accompanied a cold front as it crossed southwestern South Dakota late in the afternoon. Showers and mild temperatures helped to mix strong winds aloft to the surface as the front passed across southwestern South Dakota. Wind gusts near 60 mph were reported over portions of Fall River and Custer counties for a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system raced through the region, bringing strong northwest winds to western and south central South Dakota for a prolonged period. The initial cold front brought a period of very strong winds Saturday afternoon and evening. After the winds diminished overnight, stronger winds developed Sunday morning and persisted through the late afternoon and early evening. The strongest winds on Sunday were sustained at 35 to 50 mph with a few gusts around 75 mph, especially from northwestern into west central South Dakota.

A strong cold front crossed the region, bringing gusty northwest winds to much of western and south central South Dakota. Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph accompanied the front late on the 18th into the nighttime hours. Wind gusts around 65 mph redeveloped on the 19th across portions of northwestern South Dakota and the Black Hills as a tight pressure gradient remained in place over the Northern Plains.

A clipper system raced through the region, bringing strong northwest winds to western and south central South Dakota for a prolonged period. The initial cold front brought a period of very strong winds Saturday afternoon and evening. After the winds diminished overnight, stronger winds developed Sunday morning and persisted through the late afternoon and early evening. The strongest winds on Sunday were sustained at 35 to 50 mph with a few gusts around 75 mph, especially from northwestern into west central South Dakota.

A strong cold front sweeping across the region brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota during the early morning hours. The wind gusts were anywhere from 60 to over 80 mph. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 70 mph at Stephan; 71 mph 14 miles north of Vivian; 72 mph at Ridgeview; 75 mph 11 miles south of Bullhead; 78 mph at Pierre and near Blunt; and 83 mph 10 miles southwest of Chantier Creek in Stanley county.

A strong surface low pressure area moved from south central Canada, across northern Minnesota and into the Great Lakes Region. This brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota from the morning hours until the early evening hours of the 7th. The high winds were sustained between 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 60 to 70 mph. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 66 mph at Pierre; 67 mph near Reliance and Ridgeview; 68 mph at Whitlocks Bay and 70 mph northwest of Mclaughlin.

Snowfall of up to 14.3 inches was accompanied by north to northeast winds gusting to over 40 mph. Although the snow was wet, the strong winds contributed to the storm by causing blowing snow while the snow was falling. The snow fell from the afternoon of March 23rd to a little after midnight on March 24th. The heaviest accumulations were in the extreme southeast corner of the state. Accumulations were less to the north and northwest, dropping down to less than one inch in some areas north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranging from 14.3 inches near North Sioux City down to about 4 inches at the northwest corner of Union County was accompanied by north to northeast winds gusting to over 40 mph. Despite the wet nature of the snow, the winds caused blowing snow and drifting while the snow was falling. The snow made travel difficult to impossible at times. Several roads were closed, including Interstate 29 in the southern part of the county.

This brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota from the morning hours until the early evening hours of the 7th. The high winds were sustained between 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 60 to 70 mph. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 66 mph at Pierre; 67 mph near Reliance and Ridgeview; 68 mph at Whitlocks Bay and 70 mph northwest of Mclaughlin.

A winter storm moved across the region and brought a variety of weather including lightning and thunder, sleet, freezing rain, and heavy snow. The freezing rain and sleet were concentrated across central South Dakota. The mixed bag of weather caused travel problems across the region. Heavy snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches along with icing amounts from a few hundredths up to three tenths of an inch occurred with this storm.  Some of the snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Aberdeen, Leola, and Doland; 7 inches at Chelsea and Raymond; 8 inches at Butler and east of Lowry; 9 inches at Eagle Butte, Bowdle, and Roscoe; 10 inches east of Hosmer and 12 inches at Gettysburg. Onida received a tenth of an inch of ice with three tenths of an inch of ice at Agar.

Snowfall of up to 14.3 inches was accompanied by north to northeast winds gusting to over 40 mph. Although the snow was wet, the strong winds contributed to the storm by causing blowing snow while the snow was falling. The snow fell from the afternoon of March 23rd to a little after midnight on March 24th. The heaviest accumulations were in the extreme southeast corner of the state. Accumulations were less to the north and northwest, dropping down to less than one inch in some areas north of Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow in southeast south Dakota was accompanied by north to northwest winds reaching sustained speeds near 30 mph with gusts above 40 mph. The combination of snow and wind caused blizzard conditions from February 2nd into the predawn hours of February 3rd, with widespread visibilities near zero, bringing travel to a halt. The worst conditions were over the far southeast corner of the state where over a foot of snow fell, but enough snow fell to result in the blizzard conditions northwest to near a Springfield to Flandreau line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches, including 3 inches near Chester, combined with north to northwest winds gusting to 40 mph to lower visibilities to less than a mile in the snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from repeated heavy rain caused minor to moderate flooding of the Vermillion River in late April 2016.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from repeated rains caused minor to moderate flooding of the Vermillion River during the last three days of April. Agricultural land along the river and a few county roads were flooded. The river reached a peak stage of 2.42 feet above flood stage at Wakonda at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light showers falling into dry and very warm air resulted in several heat bursts at several locations west and northwest of Mobridge including Mahto, McLaughlin, Trail City, and to the south of Bullhead. All locations recorded sudden increases in temperatures and decreases in humidity along with spikes in wind gusts. The Mahto station rose 14 degrees in 20 minutes peaking at 96 degrees with a gust to 62 mph. At Mclaughlin, the temperature rose to 95 degrees with a 49 mph wind gust and a relative humidity of 18 percent. Although the wind only gusted to 30 mph at Trail City, the temperature still rose to 92 degrees with a relative humidity of 19 percent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat burst occurred when a sixty-two mph wind gust resulted from a light shower. The temperature rose to 90 degrees and the humidity dropped to 18 percent.

Large hail and strong wind gusts were reported across Todd and southern Tripp Counties. Two tornadoes also developed, one across southeastern Todd County and another over southwestern Tripp County. Very heavy rain also caused flooding in Colome and southeastern Tripp County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed over southeastern Todd County and moved northeastward for about ten minutes. The tornado did not damage any structures as it tracked across fields for almost five miles. However, it did snap eight to ten power poles at the ground at the intersection of Littleburg Road and Hidden Timber Road. Several large trees in a shelter belt were snapped off and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms tracked slowly northeastward from Nebraska across south central South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening. Large hail and strong wind gusts were reported across Todd and southern Tripp Counties. Two tornadoes also developed, one across southeastern Todd County and another over southwestern Tripp County. Very heavy rain also caused flooding in Colome and southeastern Tripp County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down north of Clearfield in southwestern Tripp County and tracked across a farm, causing considerable property damage. Two livestock trailers were tossed by the winds estimated near 100 mph. A pole shed was destroyed with the poles torn from the ground and metal siding and roof blown across the yard. The home had roof and siding damage. Two power poles were downed. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and very humid weather, with daytime temperatures reaching the 95 to 100 degree range, brought the heat index to 100 to 115 degrees during the afternoon hours of July 19th through July 23rd. Emergency rooms at hospitals reported that an unknown number of people suffered heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, although no lasting heat related illnesses were reported. Dew points in the 70s over the area reflected the very high humidity, with dew points at a few places peaking at or just above 80 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching daytime highs of 95 to 100 were accompanied by very humid conditions, with the heat index rising to 100 to 115 degrees. An unknown number of people suffered from heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, as reported by hospital emergency rooms.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms produced very heavy rain across southwestern South Dakota. Two to four inches of rain fell during the afternoon of July 1. Runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding along the White River through Jackson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was recorded along the White River. The river rose over ten feet and flow increased from 50 cubic feet per second (cfs) to 11,900 cfs within six hours. The river gauge on the White River near Interior crested at a height about a half foot above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and very humid weather, with daytime temperatures reaching the 95 to 100 degree range, brought the heat index to 100 to 115 degrees during the afternoon hours of July 19th through July 23rd. Emergency rooms at hospitals reported that an unknown number of people suffered heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, although no lasting heat related illnesses were reported. Dew points in the 70s over the area reflected the very high humidity, with dew points at a few places peaking at or just above 80 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching daytime highs of 95 to 100 were accompanied by very humid conditions, with the heat index rising to 100 to 115 degrees. An unknown number of people suffered from heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, as reported by hospital emergency rooms.

Snow accumulated 4 to 9 inches in most of southeast South Dakota during the day and nighttime hours of December 16th, continuing to after midnight on December 17th. Generally light winds at the start of the snowfall increased to northerly at around 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph during the night. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to dangerous levels of 20 to 25 below zero while snow was falling. The winds also caused drifting snow and areas of blowing snow. Over the far southeast corner of South Dakota, accumulations were slightly less at 3 to 5 inches, and wind chills during the snowfall dropped into the teens below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches, including 6.0 inches near Madison. Northerly winds increased to around 20 mph sustained with gusts around 30 mph, causing drifting snow and areas of blowing snow.  The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to 20 to 25 below zero during the storm. Some schools were dismissed early on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th. This storm first brought record breaking heavy rain along with heavy icing, which resulted in widespread tree and power line damage to the area. The rain and freezing rain then changed over to snow along and west of the Missouri River during the late afternoon and evening of the 25th. As the system continued moving northeast, snow moved east across the rest of the region through the morning hours of the 26th.||Many roads were closed and/or required a No Travel Advisory due to the snow and/or ice. One such road closure involved Interstate-90 from Wyoming to Chamberlain, SD on Sunday night, the 25th. Many motorists were stranded during the storm. A vehicle containing 4 adults, a child, and their dog became stuck in a ditch. The party waited overnight for a total of 12 hours in blizzard conditions before being rescued. During this time, the temperature fell from 38 degrees to 8 degrees in matter of minutes.||Snowfall amounts of 4 to 18 inches were reported along and west of the Missouri River, while generally 1 to 2 inches fell from east of the river to the Minnesota border. Some of the highest snowfall amounts include: 6 inches at Murdo and Mobridge, 8 inches at Pollock, 9 inches at Timber Lake, 13 inches 8 miles southwest of Keldron, 15 inches 6 miles southeast of McIntosh, 16 inches at McLaughlin, and 18 inches 14 miles northeast of Isabel. High wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph brought blizzard conditions to much of the region from the late afternoon on the 25th into the afternoon hours of the 26th. Specifically, winds gusted to 67 mph near Clear Lake and 70 mph at Pierre.

A couple of upper level low pressure troughs moving over the region brought heavy snow to parts of central and northeast South Dakota from the morning of the 10th to the morning of the 11th. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches occurred across the region. Some amounts include, 6 inches at Chelsea, Estelline, Clark, Milbank, Doland, and Redfield; 7 inches at Turton, Orient, Seneca, Castlewood, Wilmot, and Ree Heights; 8 inches at Hayti, La Bolt, Summit, and Watertown; and 9 inches at Rauville, Big Stone City, and Clear Lake.

Snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches occurred across the region. Some amounts include, 6 inches at Chelsea, Estelline, Clark, Milbank, Doland, and Redfield; 7 inches at Turton, Orient, Seneca, Castlewood, Wilmot, and Ree Heights; 8 inches at Hayti, La Bolt, Summit, and Watertown; and 9 inches at Rauville, Big Stone City, and Clear Lake.

An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th. This storm was unusually warm for the region for late December and produced record breaking heavy rain along with flooding in some cases. Significant icing occurred across areas at or just below the freezing point, which resulted in widespread tree and power pole and line damage to the area. Some downed branches and trees fell onto homes across the region. This storm also brought high winds along with snow and blizzard conditions to the region. This significant storm resulted in massive power outages, stranded motorists and closed roads. ||Ice accumulations were significant across central and northeastern South Dakota with over an inch accumulation for some locations. High winds during this event increased the amount of power pole, line, and tree damage. Those who did not see freezing rain accumulations had to deal with ice as well. The ponding of the heavy rain froze overnight once much colder air moved in. Roads and walkways became treacherous ice rinks and remained as such for many days. There were numerous injuries from slips on the ice, as well as several vehicular accidents and flight cancellations. Livestock was also affected, though most made it through the storm. Dairy operations dealt with frozen drinking water tanks.||Precipitation amounts were very impressive for late December, as the system had near record levels of atmospheric moisture to work with. Rain or freezing rain was the predominant precipitation type for those roughly east of the Missouri River on the 25th. Some of the heaviest rainfall amounts include: 0.82 inches at Sisseton and Summit, 0.99 inches at Eureka, 1.05 inches at Miller, 1.11 inches at Leola, 1.14 inches at Victor, 1.28 inches at Aberdeen, 1.34 inches at Orient, 1.44 inches at Bath, 1.48 inches at Westport and 1.73 inches southwest of Stratford. From this rainfall, ice accumulation amounts ranged from a quarter inch to nearly an inch and a half in places. The highest measured ice accumulation was 1.37 inches about 2 miles east of Summit.||Daily precipitation records were set on the 25th for Mobridge with 1.52 inches, Pierre with 0.92 of an inch, Aberdeen with 1.28 inches, and Sisseton with 0.7 of an inch. Aberdeen and Mobridge also broke records for their wettest December day on record, while Pierre recording their 2nd wettest December day on record. Finally, Mobridge also broke their record for the wettest winter day (December through February) with Aberdeen recording their 3rd wettest winter day on record. In Aberdeen, the heavy rainfall combined with melting snow and ice to produce flooding across several streets, driveways, and garages. No doubt a remarkable amount of moisture for a late December winter storm system. Rare thunderstorms, more indicative of spring than winter, were also widely reported on both the rain and snow side of this system across the area on the 25th.||High winds gusting to over 70 mph impacted the entire region on the 25th and 26th. The combination of snow and ice and high winds snapped or otherwise damaged hundreds of power poles, downed several thousand miles of power lines, damaged several hundred transmission structures and brought many substations down. Many roads were blocked by power lines.  Overall, more than one hundred lineman worked to bring the power back. ||Twenty-one counties encompassing 30 communities and 3 Indian reservations were impacted. Entire communities, thousands of homes and businesses, and ultimately over 12,000 people went without power. For some, power was not restored for 10 days despite tireless efforts. All power was restored by January 4th, 2017. Water and sewer systems shut down for several days for some communities and emergency shelters were necessary. Deuel, Day, Marshall, Roberts and Grant counties were the hardest hit. County and city governments were overwhelmed by ice accumulations and blizzard conditions and struggled with maintaining accessibility even for emergency traffic. Road conditions deteriorated to the point where it took up to several hours for emergency officials to respond to 911 calls. The storm proved to be fatal when a Walworth County man fell, hit his head and succumbed to the elements on Christmas Day.  ||National Weather Service operations were impacted as well. The surface observing station in Aberdeen and Mobridge were adversely affected or went completely down during the storm. Additionally, the upper air shelter, which houses weather balloons, was frozen shut and thus inaccessible for the morning launch on the 26th.||Due to widespread significant impacts, the Governor of South Dakota declared a State of Emergency on the 26th which helped facilitate the movement of out-of-state crews to aid with power restoration. There was also a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public property. The total estimated damage was near 8 million dollars for central and northeast South Dakota.

Snow accumulated 4 to 9 inches in most of southeast South Dakota during the day and nighttime hours of December 16th, continuing to after midnight on December 17th. Generally light winds at the start of the snowfall increased to northerly at around 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph during the night. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to dangerous levels of 20 to 25 below zero while snow was falling. The winds also caused drifting snow and areas of blowing snow. Over the far southeast corner of South Dakota, accumulations were slightly less at 3 to 5 inches, and wind chills during the snowfall dropped into the teens below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 9 inches, including 8.6 inches near Tea. Northerly winds increased to around 20 mph sustained with gusts around 30 mph, causing drifting snow and areas of blowing snow.  The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to near 20 below zero during the storm. Some schools were dismissed early on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th, and produced high winds along the way. During the late evening and early morning hours from the 25th into the 26th, high sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph developed with gusts to over 70 mph. Some of the highest gusts include 62 mph near Stephan, 70 mph in Pierre, 71 mph near Danforth and 72 mph at Reliance. The storm also brought ice storm and blizzard conditions mainly to the northwest and northeast parts of South Dakota. All standing water also froze once much colder air moved in, which made roads and walkways treacherous.

An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th. This storm first brought record breaking heavy rain along with heavy icing, which resulted in widespread tree and power line damage to the area. The rain and freezing rain then changed over to snow along and west of the Missouri River during the late afternoon and evening of the 25th. As the system continued moving northeast, snow moved east across the rest of the region through the morning hours of the 26th.||Many roads were closed and/or required a No Travel Advisory due to the snow and/or ice. One such road closure involved Interstate-90 from Wyoming to Chamberlain, SD on Sunday night, the 25th. Many motorists were stranded during the storm. A vehicle containing 4 adults, a child, and their dog became stuck in a ditch. The party waited overnight for a total of 12 hours in blizzard conditions before being rescued. During this time, the temperature fell from 38 degrees to 8 degrees in matter of minutes.||Snowfall amounts of 4 to 18 inches were reported along and west of the Missouri River, while generally 1 to 2 inches fell from east of the river to the Minnesota border. Some of the highest snowfall amounts include: 6 inches at Murdo and Mobridge, 8 inches at Pollock, 9 inches at Timber Lake, 13 inches 8 miles southwest of Keldron, 15 inches 6 miles southeast of McIntosh, 16 inches at McLaughlin, and 18 inches 14 miles northeast of Isabel. High wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph brought blizzard conditions to much of the region from the late afternoon on the 25th into the afternoon hours of the 26th. Specifically, winds gusted to 67 mph near Clear Lake and 70 mph at Pierre.

From this rainfall, ice accumulation amounts ranged from a quarter inch to nearly an inch and a half in places. The highest measured ice accumulation was 1.37 inches about 2 miles east of Summit.||Daily precipitation records were set on the 25th for Mobridge with 1.52 inches, Pierre with 0.92 of an inch, Aberdeen with 1.28 inches, and Sisseton with 0.7 of an inch. Aberdeen and Mobridge also broke records for their wettest December day on record, while Pierre recording their 2nd wettest December day on record. Finally, Mobridge also broke their record for the wettest winter day (December through February) with Aberdeen recording their 3rd wettest winter day on record. In Aberdeen, the heavy rainfall combined with melting snow and ice to produce flooding across several streets, driveways, and garages. No doubt a remarkable amount of moisture for a late December winter storm system. Rare thunderstorms, more indicative of spring than winter, were also widely reported on both the rain and snow side of this system across the area on the 25th.||High winds gusting to over 70 mph impacted the entire region on the 25th and 26th. The combination of snow and ice and high winds snapped or otherwise damaged hundreds of power poles, downed several thousand miles of power lines, damaged several hundred transmission structures and brought many substations down. Many roads were blocked by power lines.  Overall, more than one hundred lineman worked to bring the power back. ||Twenty-one counties encompassing 30 communities and 3 Indian reservations were impacted. Entire communities, thousands of homes and businesses, and ultimately over 12,000 people went without power. For some, power was not restored for 10 days despite tireless efforts. All power was restored by January 4th, 2017. Water and sewer systems shut down for several days for some communities and emergency shelters were necessary. Deuel, Day, Marshall, Roberts and Grant counties were the hardest hit. County and city governments were overwhelmed by ice accumulations and blizzard conditions and struggled with maintaining accessibility even for emergency traffic. Road conditions deteriorated to the point where it took up to several hours for emergency officials to respond to 911 calls. The storm proved to be fatal when a Walworth County man fell, hit his head and succumbed to the elements on Christmas Day.  ||National Weather Service operations were impacted as well. The surface observing station in Aberdeen and Mobridge were adversely affected or went completely down during the storm. Additionally, the upper air shelter, which houses weather balloons, was frozen shut and thus inaccessible for the morning launch on the 26th.||Due to widespread significant impacts, the Governor of South Dakota declared a State of Emergency on the 26th which helped facilitate the movement of out-of-state crews to aid with power restoration. There was also a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public property. The total estimated damage was near 8 million dollars for central and northeast South Dakota.

Additionally, the upper air shelter, which houses weather balloons, was frozen shut and thus inaccessible for the morning launch on the 26th.||Due to widespread significant impacts, the Governor of South Dakota declared a State of Emergency on the 26th which helped facilitate the movement of out-of-state crews to aid with power restoration. There was also a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public property. The total estimated damage was near 8 million dollars for central and northeast South Dakota.

An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th. This storm was unusually warm for the region for late December and produced record breaking heavy rain along with flooding in some cases. Significant icing occurred across areas at or just below the freezing point, which resulted in widespread tree and power pole and line damage to the area. Some downed branches and trees fell onto homes across the region. This storm also brought high winds along with snow and blizzard conditions to the region. This significant storm resulted in massive power outages, stranded motorists and closed roads. ||Ice accumulations were significant across central and northeastern South Dakota with over an inch accumulation for some locations. High winds during this event increased the amount of power pole, line, and tree damage. Those who did not see freezing rain accumulations had to deal with ice as well. The ponding of the heavy rain froze overnight once much colder air moved in. Roads and walkways became treacherous ice rinks and remained as such for many days. There were numerous injuries from slips on the ice, as well as several vehicular accidents and flight cancellations. Livestock was also affected, though most made it through the storm. Dairy operations dealt with frozen drinking water tanks.||Precipitation amounts were very impressive for late December, as the system had near record levels of atmospheric moisture to work with. Rain or freezing rain was the predominant precipitation type for those roughly east of the Missouri River on the 25th. Some of the heaviest rainfall amounts include: 0.82 inches at Sisseton and Summit, 0.99 inches at Eureka, 1.05 inches at Miller, 1.11 inches at Leola, 1.14 inches at Victor, 1.28 inches at Aberdeen, 1.34 inches at Orient, 1.44 inches at Bath, 1.48 inches at Westport and 1.73 inches southwest of Stratford. From this rainfall, ice accumulation amounts ranged from a quarter inch to nearly an inch and a half in places. The highest measured ice accumulation was 1.37 inches about 2 miles east of Summit.||Daily precipitation records were set on the 25th for Mobridge with 1.52 inches, Pierre with 0.92 of an inch, Aberdeen with 1.28 inches, and Sisseton with 0.7 of an inch. Aberdeen and Mobridge also broke records for their wettest December day on record, while Pierre recording their 2nd wettest December day on record. Finally, Mobridge also broke their record for the wettest winter day (December through February) with Aberdeen recording their 3rd wettest winter day on record. In Aberdeen, the heavy rainfall combined with melting snow and ice to produce flooding across several streets, driveways, and garages. No doubt a remarkable amount of moisture for a late December winter storm system. Rare thunderstorms, more indicative of spring than winter, were also widely reported on both the rain and snow side of this system across the area on the 25th.||High winds gusting to over 70 mph impacted the entire region on the 25th and 26th. The combination of snow and ice and high winds snapped or otherwise damaged hundreds of power poles, downed several thousand miles of power lines, damaged several hundred transmission structures and brought many substations down. Many roads were blocked by power lines.  Overall, more than one hundred lineman worked to bring the power back. ||Twenty-one counties encompassing 30 communities and 3 Indian reservations were impacted. Entire communities, thousands of homes and businesses, and ultimately over 12,000 people went without power. For some, power was not restored for 10 days despite tireless efforts. All power was restored by January 4th, 2017. Water and sewer systems shut down for several days for some communities and emergency shelters were necessary. Deuel, Day, Marshall, Roberts and Grant counties were the hardest hit. County and city governments were overwhelmed by ice accumulations and blizzard conditions and struggled with maintaining accessibility even for emergency traffic. Road conditions deteriorated to the point where it took up to several hours for emergency officials to respond to 911 calls. The storm proved to be fatal when a Walworth County man fell, hit his head and succumbed to the elements on Christmas Day.  ||National Weather Service operations were impacted as well. The surface observing station in Aberdeen and Mobridge were adversely affected or went completely down during the storm. Additionally, the upper air shelter, which houses weather balloons, was frozen shut and thus inaccessible for the morning launch on the 26th.||Due to widespread significant impacts, the Governor of South Dakota declared a State of Emergency on the 26th which helped facilitate the movement of out-of-state crews to aid with power restoration. There was also a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public property. The total estimated damage was near 8 million dollars for central and northeast South Dakota.

Snow accumulated 4 to 9 inches in most of southeast South Dakota during the day and nighttime hours of December 16th, continuing to after midnight on December 17th. Generally light winds at the start of the snowfall increased to northerly at around 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph during the night. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to dangerous levels of 20 to 25 below zero while snow was falling. The winds also caused drifting snow and areas of blowing snow. Over the far southeast corner of South Dakota, accumulations were slightly less at 3 to 5 inches, and wind chills during the snowfall dropped into the teens below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches, including 6.0 inches at Huron. Northerly winds increased to over 20 mph sustained with gusts over 30 mph, causing drifting snow and areas of blowing snow.  The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to around 25 below zero during the storm. Some schools were dismissed early on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moved across the Central Plains and brought heavy snow to parts of central and northeast South Dakota from the morning hours of the 16th to the morning hours of the 17th. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches occurred across the region. Travel was significantly affected with many roads snow covered or drifted over. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, Gettysburg, Watertown, Gann Valley, Seneca, and Pierre; 7 inches at Miller, Fort Pierre, Garden City, Kennebec, and Presho; 8 inches at Agar and Oacoma; and 10 inches at Murdo.

An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th, and produced high winds along the way. During the late evening and early morning hours from the 25th into the 26th, high sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph developed with gusts to over 70 mph. Some of the highest gusts include 62 mph near Stephan, 70 mph in Pierre, 71 mph near Danforth and 72 mph at Reliance. The storm also brought ice storm and blizzard conditions mainly to the northwest and northeast parts of South Dakota. All standing water also froze once much colder air moved in, which made roads and walkways treacherous.

An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th. This storm first brought record breaking heavy rain along with heavy icing, which resulted in widespread tree and power line damage to the area. The rain and freezing rain then changed over to snow along and west of the Missouri River during the late afternoon and evening of the 25th. As the system continued moving northeast, snow moved east across the rest of the region through the morning hours of the 26th.||Many roads were closed and/or required a No Travel Advisory due to the snow and/or ice. One such road closure involved Interstate-90 from Wyoming to Chamberlain, SD on Sunday night, the 25th. Many motorists were stranded during the storm. A vehicle containing 4 adults, a child, and their dog became stuck in a ditch. The party waited overnight for a total of 12 hours in blizzard conditions before being rescued. During this time, the temperature fell from 38 degrees to 8 degrees in matter of minutes.||Snowfall amounts of 4 to 18 inches were reported along and west of the Missouri River, while generally 1 to 2 inches fell from east of the river to the Minnesota border. Some of the highest snowfall amounts include: 6 inches at Murdo and Mobridge, 8 inches at Pollock, 9 inches at Timber Lake, 13 inches 8 miles southwest of Keldron, 15 inches 6 miles southeast of McIntosh, 16 inches at McLaughlin, and 18 inches 14 miles northeast of Isabel. High wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph brought blizzard conditions to much of the region from the late afternoon on the 25th into the afternoon hours of the 26th. Specifically, winds gusted to 67 mph near Clear Lake and 70 mph at Pierre.

Rare late December heavy rains of over 1.25 inches fell on top of the existing snow cover to bring flashing flooding to Aberdeen. Many storm drains became clogged from slush which caused a number of streets to flood. Water also backed up into some homes, flooding several basements. A No Travel Advisory was issued for the area.

Strong winds, which were slowly decreasing after a snowfall, combined with bitterly cold arctic air to drive wind chills to 35 to 50 degrees below zero from late on December 17th through the morning of December 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds on December 17th, decreasing to around 10 mph during the night of the 17th, combined with falling temperatures to lower wind chill readings to 40 to 50 below zero. Actual temperatures dropped to colder than 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moved across the Central Plains and brought heavy snow to parts of central and northeast South Dakota from the morning hours of the 16th to the morning hours of the 17th. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches occurred across the region. Travel was significantly affected with many roads snow covered or drifted over. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, Gettysburg, Watertown, Gann Valley, Seneca, and Pierre; 7 inches at Miller, Fort Pierre, Garden City, Kennebec, and Presho; 8 inches at Agar and Oacoma; and 10 inches at Murdo.

After the fresh snowfall from the day before, bitter cold Arctic air built into the region Saturday and Sunday bringing record low temperatures along with extreme wind chills to all of the region. Record lows in the 20s and 30s below zero occurred on both Saturday night before midnight and Sunday Morning at several locations. Both Aberdeen and Watertown broke their record low by several degrees on Sunday morning with both falling to 37 degrees below zero. This Arctic air combined with 10 to 20 mph winds brought wind chills to 35 to 60 degrees below zero across the region. Many church services were cancelled on Sunday. Some bitter wind chills include, 36 degrees below at Pierre, 40 degrees below at Sisseton, 43 degrees below at Mobridge, 48 degrees below at Watertown, and 58 degrees below zero at Aberdeen.

An intense surface low pressure area moved from northeast Colorado to South Dakota from the 24th through the 26th. This storm first brought record breaking heavy rain along with heavy icing, which resulted in widespread tree and power line damage to the area. The rain and freezing rain then changed over to snow along and west of the Missouri River during the late afternoon and evening of the 25th. As the system continued moving northeast, snow moved east across the rest of the region through the morning hours of the 26th.||Many roads were closed and/or required a No Travel Advisory due to the snow and/or ice. One such road closure involved Interstate-90 from Wyoming to Chamberlain, SD on Sunday night, the 25th. Many motorists were stranded during the storm. A vehicle containing 4 adults, a child, and their dog became stuck in a ditch. The party waited overnight for a total of 12 hours in blizzard conditions before being rescued. During this time, the temperature fell from 38 degrees to 8 degrees in matter of minutes.||Snowfall amounts of 4 to 18 inches were reported along and west of the Missouri River, while generally 1 to 2 inches fell from east of the river to the Minnesota border. Some of the highest snowfall amounts include: 6 inches at Murdo and Mobridge, 8 inches at Pollock, 9 inches at Timber Lake, 13 inches 8 miles southwest of Keldron, 15 inches 6 miles southeast of McIntosh, 16 inches at McLaughlin, and 18 inches 14 miles northeast of Isabel. High wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph brought blizzard conditions to much of the region from the late afternoon on the 25th into the afternoon hours of the 26th. Specifically, winds gusted to 67 mph near Clear Lake and 70 mph at Pierre.

Strong winds, which were slowly decreasing after a snowfall, combined with bitterly cold arctic air to drive wind chills to 35 to 50 degrees below zero from late on December 17th through the morning of December 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds on December 17th, decreasing to around 10 mph during the night of the 17th, combined with falling temperatures to lower wind chill readings to 40 to 50 below zero. Actual temperatures dropped to colder than 20 below zero, including a record 25 below zero at White Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed over southeast South Dakota on Christmas night and continued into the morning of December 26th. The southwest to west winds developed as a low pressure system, which had brought rain and thunderstorms on Christmas Day, moved just north of the area. Sustained winds reached up to 50 mph, and gusts were measured as high as 72 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. There were several reports of power lines and branches down and resulting power outages, and traffic lights and signs were damaged. The winds included a measured peak gust of 66 mph at Vermillion Airport.

Very strong winds developed over southeast South Dakota on Christmas night and continued into the morning of December 26th. The southwest to west winds developed as a low pressure system, which had brought rain and thunderstorms on Christmas Day, moved just north of the area. Sustained winds reached up to 50 mph, and gusts were measured as high as 72 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. There were several reports of power lines and branches down and resulting power outages, and traffic lights and signs were damaged. The winds included a measured peak gust of 72 mph at Yankton Airport.

An intense storm system moved across the Northern Plains, producing snow and strong winds across much of western South Dakota. Areas of freezing rain, freezing drizzle, sleet, and snow developed over the area in the morning, before changing to snow. Snowfall was heaviest during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow were reported across much of western South Dakota, with local amounts as much as a foot. Little or no snow fell across south central South Dakota, where temperatures were warm enough for mostly rain. Embedded thunder was also noted with the precipitation. Strong winds developed late in the day and continued into the next morning, with blizzard conditions across much of the western South Dakota plains. Interstate 90 was closed for a prolonged period from the Wyoming border to Chamberlain. Rural electric systems sustained significant damage to poles and wires due the snow, ice, and wind. A man died as he tried to walk home after his pickup truck went into a ditch.

An intense storm system moved across the Northern Plains, producing snow and strong winds across much of western South Dakota. Areas of freezing rain, freezing drizzle, sleet, and snow developed over the area in the morning, before changing to snow. Snowfall was heaviest during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow were reported across much of western South Dakota, with local amounts as much as a foot. Little or no snow fell across south central South Dakota, where temperatures were warm enough for mostly rain. Embedded thunder was also noted with the precipitation. Strong winds developed late in the day and continued into the next morning, with blizzard conditions across much of the western South Dakota plains. Interstate 90 was closed for a prolonged period from the Wyoming border to Chamberlain. Rural electric systems sustained significant damage to poles and wires due the snow, ice, and wind. A man died as he tried to walk home after his pickup truck went into a ditch.

Little or no snow fell across south central South Dakota, where temperatures were warm enough for mostly rain. Embedded thunder was also noted with the precipitation. Strong winds developed late in the day and continued into the next morning, with blizzard conditions across much of the western South Dakota plains. Interstate 90 was closed for a prolonged period from the Wyoming border to Chamberlain. Rural electric systems sustained significant damage to poles and wires due the snow, ice, and wind. A man died as he tried to walk home after his pickup truck went into a ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed over southeast South Dakota on Christmas night and continued into the morning of December 26th. The southwest to west winds developed as a low pressure system, which had brought rain and thunderstorms on Christmas Day, moved just north of the area. Sustained winds reached up to 50 mph, and gusts were measured as high as 72 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. There were several reports of power lines and branches down and resulting power outages. The winds included measured sustained winds of 40 mph and a peak gust of 53 mph at Huron, but stronger winds and gusts were suspected to have occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moved across South Dakota bringing 1 to 6 inches of snow to north central South Dakota. Arctic air surging in behind brought very strong northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph with gusts to around 55 mph. The winds combined with snow brought blizzard conditions to much of north central South Dakota from early afternoon to the early evening. Travel was significantly impacted or halted by the very low visibility. Snowfall amounts include, 2 inches at Timber Lake, 3 inches at McIntosh, 5 inches northeast of Isabel, and 6 inches at McLaughlin.

A surface low pressure area moved across the Central Plains and brought heavy snow to parts of central and northeast South Dakota from the morning hours of the 16th to the morning hours of the 17th. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches occurred across the region. Travel was significantly affected with many roads snow covered or drifted over. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, Gettysburg, Watertown, Gann Valley, Seneca, and Pierre; 7 inches at Miller, Fort Pierre, Garden City, Kennebec, and Presho; 8 inches at Agar and Oacoma; and 10 inches at Murdo.

Areas of freezing rain, freezing drizzle, sleet, and snow developed over the area in the morning, before changing to snow. Snowfall was heaviest during the late afternoon and nighttime hours. Four to eight inches of snow were reported across much of western South Dakota, with local amounts as much as a foot. Little or no snow fell across south central South Dakota, where temperatures were warm enough for mostly rain. Embedded thunder was also noted with the precipitation. Strong winds developed late in the day and continued into the next morning, with blizzard conditions across much of the western South Dakota plains. Interstate 90 was closed for a prolonged period from the Wyoming border to Chamberlain. Rural electric systems sustained significant damage to poles and wires due the snow, ice, and wind. A man died as he tried to walk home after his pickup truck went into a ditch.

A very warm and abnormally large upper level high pressure area along with high dew points brought high heat indices to central and northeast South Dakota. High temperatures were in the upper 80s to the 100s with overnight lows in the upper 60s to the mid 70s. A few of the highest heat index values include: 105 degrees at Britton, 106 degrees at Sisseton and Watertown, 107 degrees at Pierre, 108 degrees at Aberdeen and Clark, 109 degrees at Mobridge, 110 degrees at Eureka and Miller and 111 degrees at Clear Lake.

Rain changed to snow late on November 17th, and wet snow accumulated up to 9.5 inches through November 18th. Northwest winds gusting as high as 56 mph combined with the falling snow to lower visibilities to below a quarter mile at times, making travel difficult, especially since most of the storm occurred at night. Wind chills were driven down into the teens, not an unusual level for mid November. However, the change to colder weather was sudden, as unseasonably warm weather had brought daytime temperatures as warm as the 60s and 70s within the previous five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 4 to 9.5 inches, including 9.5 inches at Parker. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to 50 mph, which combined with the falling snow to produce visibilities less than a quarter mile at times. Travel was difficult because of the low visibility and slippery surfaces. Wind chills were driven down into the teens after an immediately preceding period of much above normal temperatures. Classes were delayed or cancelled at many schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front in very unstable air in west central South Dakota.  These storms merged together into a small severe bow as they moved east into central South Dakota. As the bow moved east, damaging winds from 60 to over 80 mph occurred across central South Dakota through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seventy mph winds moved a mobile home three to four feet off of its foundation.

A strong low pressure system east of the region produced strong winds over portions of western South Dakota, with the strongest winds in the Rapid City area. Northwesterly winds of 30 to 45 mph with a few gusts around 60 mph were recorded from Rapid City to Hermosa.

A strong low pressure system moved into eastern portions of the Northern Plains and stalled out for nearly 48 hours. Wrap-around moisture behind the low brought snow to much of western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills as significant upslope snowfall developed and persisted for 36 hours. Snowfall amounts of one to two feet were common across the northern Black Hills, with localized amounts to three feet west of Lead. Snow amounts on the northwestern to west central South Dakota plains ranged from three to seven inches, with little to no snowfall from the Rapid City area to far southwestern South Dakota. Strong northwesterly winds caused significant blowing and drifting snow and visibilities below a quarter mile in many areas. Interstate 90 was closed from Piedmont to Spearfish on the afternoon of the 29th to the morning of the 30th.

A very warm and abnormally large upper level high pressure area along with high dew points brought high heat indices to central and northeast South Dakota. High temperatures were in the upper 80s to the 100s with overnight lows in the upper 60s to the mid 70s. A few of the highest heat index values include: 105 degrees at Britton, 106 degrees at Sisseton and Watertown, 107 degrees at Pierre, 108 degrees at Aberdeen and Clark, 109 degrees at Mobridge, 110 degrees at Eureka and Miller and 111 degrees at Clear Lake.

A strong low pressure system moved into eastern portions of the Northern Plains and stalled out for nearly 48 hours. Wrap-around moisture behind the low brought snow to much of western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills as significant upslope snowfall developed and persisted for 36 hours. Snowfall amounts of one to two feet were common across the northern Black Hills, with localized amounts to three feet west of Lead. Snow amounts on the northwestern to west central South Dakota plains ranged from three to seven inches, with little to no snowfall from the Rapid City area to far southwestern South Dakota. Strong northwesterly winds caused significant blowing and drifting snow and visibilities below a quarter mile in many areas. Interstate 90 was closed from Piedmont to Spearfish on the afternoon of the 29th to the morning of the 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds downed several tree branches along a tree row with a few branches thrown east across the road into a corn field.  The corn stocks were leaning 45 degrees to the east.

Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front extending across northern South Dakota and also along a weak trough axis extending across central South Dakota. Damaging winds up to eighty mph along with large hail were a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated over seventy mph overturned three RV campers at Elm Lake. All three campers were on a hill overlooking the lake. One camper rolled one to two times down a small valley.  The second camper turned over onto its side.  The last camper rolled over just a half turn and then was pushed approximately 100 to 150 feet around the side of the hill and down toward the lake.  Numerous boats and pontoons were also flipped over in the water.  Boat lifts and canopies could be seen flipped over with canopies torn.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along with an outbuilding being severely damaged by seventy-five mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a wind shift line bringing some severe winds along with a few funnel clouds to parts of north central and northeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was broke off at the trunk along with another tree partially uprooted. Many other large branches were downed in the shelter belt with minor damage to the edge of the cornfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts at 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving severe thunderstorm developed over the northern foothills of the Black Hills and drifted slowly southeast to the Rapid City area. Very large hail and strong wind gusts were reported from Piedmont to the north side of Rapid City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into eastern portions of the Northern Plains and stalled out for nearly 48 hours. Wrap-around moisture behind the low brought snow to much of western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills as significant upslope snowfall developed and persisted for 36 hours. Snowfall amounts of one to two feet were common across the northern Black Hills, with localized amounts to three feet west of Lead. Snow amounts on the northwestern to west central South Dakota plains ranged from three to seven inches, with little to no snowfall from the Rapid City area to far southwestern South Dakota. Strong northwesterly winds caused significant blowing and drifting snow and visibilities below a quarter mile in many areas. Interstate 90 was closed from Piedmont to Spearfish on the afternoon of the 29th to the morning of the 30th.

Rain changed to snow late on November 17th, and wet snow accumulated up to 9.5 inches through November 18th. Northwest winds gusting as high as 56 mph combined with the falling snow to lower visibilities to below a quarter mile at times, making travel difficult, especially since most of the storm occurred at night. Wind chills were driven down into the teens, not an unusual level for mid November. However, the change to colder weather was sudden, as unseasonably warm weather had brought daytime temperatures as warm as the 60s and 70s within the previous five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches, including 4 inches about 2 miles south of Huron. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds gusting  to 45 mph, which combined with the falling snow to produce visibilities less than a quarter mile at times. Travel was difficult because of the low visibility and slippery surfaces. Wind chills were driven down into the teens after an immediately preceding period of much above normal temperatures. Classes were delayed or cancelled at many schools.

A strong surface low pressure area moved northeast across the region and brought snowfall from 2 to 7 inches to northeast South Dakota. Along with the snow, strong northwest sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 to 70 mph brought blizzard conditions to all of the region from the morning into the afternoon hours. The highest snowfall amount was near Watertown with 7 inches and the highest wind gusts were 61 mph near Estelline and 71 mph at Summit. There was a No Travel Advisory issued for a few counties with whiteout conditions reported along I-29. Many schools were also cancelled. The snowfall began between 4 and 6 in the morning and ended by 4 in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was accompanied by moderate winds, with peak gusts slightly more than 20 mph. The snow was heavy in part of Brookings County, otherwise accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were reported in 18 to 24 hours, mostly in the area east of the James River. The snow began the morning of December 10th, and continued into the early morning of December 11th. Visibility was occasionally lowered to below a mile by the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches, including 3.2 inches at Marion. Winds were moderate, gusting at times to around 20 mph, and temperatures were well below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph occurred during the early morning hours southwest of Peever on Interstate-29.

Very warm, dry, and breezy conditions contributed to a wildfire five miles northeast of Reliance. This fire developed due to a spark from a car fire on November 3rd. The fire burned 750 acres or almost a full square mile. It started around 2 pm and was not completely put out until 10 pm. Fifty firefighters from seven fire departments along with several farmers helped extinguish the fire. Some structures were threatened. The fire did force the closure of a 3-mile stretch of South Dakota Highway 47 for over five hours.

Travel was difficult because of the low visibility and snow covered surfaces. Wind chills were driven down into the teens after an immediately preceding period of much above normal  temperatures. Classes were cancelled at many schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow late on November 17th, and wet snow accumulated up to 9.5 inches through November 18th. Northwest winds gusting as high as 56 mph combined with the falling snow to lower visibilities to below a quarter mile at times, making travel difficult, especially since most of the storm occurred at night. Wind chills were driven down into the teens, not an unusual level for mid November. However, the change to colder weather was sudden, as unseasonably warm weather had brought daytime temperatures as warm as the 60s and 70s within the previous five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds gusting  to 50 mph, which combined with the falling snow to produce visibilities less than a quarter mile at times. Travel was difficult because of the low visibility and slippery surfaces. Wind chills were driven down into the teens after an immediately preceding period of much above normal temperatures. Classes were delayed or cancelled at many schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the southwestern Black Hills, producing wind gusts around 60 mph west of Jewel Cave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts reached 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow was accompanied by moderate winds, with peak gusts slightly more than 20 mph. The snow was heavy in part of Brookings County, otherwise accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were reported in 18 to 24 hours, mostly in the area east of the James River. The snow began the morning of December 10th, and continued into the early morning of December 11th. Visibility was occasionally lowered to below a mile by the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches, including 8 inches one mile southwest of Brookings. Winds were moderate, gusting at times to around 20 mph, and temperatures were well below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe storm clusters developed along a warm front in a very unstable environment. Damaging winds up to 80 mph, large hail up to tennis ball size, and an isolated tornado occurred.

A slow moving severe thunderstorm developed over northwestern Harding County and tracked southeast across the county. The storm produced hail to golf ball size and strong wind gusts. Three to five inches of rain over the northern part of the county caused flooding of gravel roads, especially between Ladner and Ludlow.

A strong storm system moved quickly across the region, producing accumulating snow across all of western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills area, where four to eight inches of snow was reported. Three to five inches of snow fell across portions of southwestern and south central South Dakota, with visibilities below a quarter mile at times due to blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moved across the region bringing Arctic air and high winds to parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph gusting to 60 to 70 mph occurred. The winds combined with snowfall also brought some low visibility affecting travel.

A strong surface low pressure area moved across the region bringing Arctic air and high winds to parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph gusting to 60 to 70 mph occurred. The winds combined with snowfall also brought some low visibility affecting travel.

High winds occurred on the backside of a retreating surface high pressure area to the lee side of the Sisseton Hills. Sixty mph plus winds along with the surface snow brought whiteout conditions on I-29 southwest of Peever for a short period of time.
A second thunderstorm briefly became severe over northwestern Ziebach County, producing 60 mph wind gusts southwest of Glad Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts of 60 mph with the storm.
